narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.20 and strong early performance placing you in the 8th decile for Year 1 grades at your school, we appreciate the effort you've put into your academic journey so far. Your family comes from an upper-middle income background, and you've indicated plans to pursue full-time studies, which aligns with the majority of applicants. However, the admissions model, designed to predict the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your probability of failure at 43%, which unfortunately crossed the threshold leading to the rejection of your law school application. This prediction was driven primarily by your LSAT score of 26, which stood out as the most influential factor pushing the model toward forecasting a higher risk of bar failure compared to typical applicants who average around 36.7. On a more positive note, your Year 1 decile ranking of 8—well above the average of 5.71—strongly worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success more than any other positive element. Your undergraduate GPA, right around the dataset average of 3.21, also helped nudge the outlook toward passing, showing consistency in your academic record. Less influential but still supportive were your family income bracket and full-time enrollment plans, both of which slightly favored a positive bar outcome relative to peers. In contrast, your Year 3 decile of 3, below the average of 5.53, had a modest negative pull, hinting at some later dips in performance. Overall, while your early strengths and background are commendable, the LSAT score was the standout concern when stacked against typical successful applicants. To move forward, we recommend retaking the LSAT to boost your score, as even a modest improvement could significantly shift future predictions in your favor. You might also reflect on what led to the Year 3 decile drop and address it through targeted preparation or additional coursework. We're rooting for you—many students in similar situations have succeeded by focusing on these key areas and reapplying stronger. Keep pushing; your potential is clear.
